speaker: interviewer
text: Gerald Steinberg. Interviewer is Donna Kenny. We are in [INAUDIBLE], Mass., USA, and the language is English. Please continue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gerry, you were saying that you remember the day in the DP camp that you said goodbye to your adopted sister. What do you remember about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. What little I remember at that time, we were already earmarked to emigrate to the United States. And she, along with my aunt and uncle and their son, were going to Israel. And I remember being woken up early in the morning, saying-- you know, by my parents, and saying for Aviva that it was time for her to go and so forth. And I remember crying about it and so forth, being separated. I don't remember her crying, but I remember my being briefly upset about it, the fact that we were being separated.

speaker: interviewer
text: And soon after that experience, you and your mother and father immigrated to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that voyage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We-- we, of course, left the DP camp in Pocking. And at various stages, you were kind of processed, if you will, you know, papers checked and so forth during the journey. And so I remember going to Hamburg, I believe. It was another city in Germany. And again, we were-- went through a brief stay and whatever credentials were checked during that time. And then from there we went to Bremerhaven. That was the port that we left by ship to come to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your port of entry in the United States was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was New York, and the name of the ship was USS General Holbrook.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened after you arrived in New York? Did you settle in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to New York. No, we came to New York on-- early on a Saturday morning. I guess we arrived during the night, and the ship was unable to dock. So it was kind of anchored in the waters of New York Harbor. And I remember being out on the deck and seeing the-- all the lights, the green lights and red lights and the lights from New York that were just overwhelming at the time. And of course, I had no idea what they were. And it was just very exciting for me. I remember staying up all night and watching that. And in the morning, we docked. And I remember being processed on the docks in very large building. It must have been Ellis Island. And I remember being dressed up in, like, green khaki outfit with a soldier's cap, so to speak. And I remember being given donuts. And it was a tremendously large hall on the docks. And after we were processed, my cousins here in Springfield had arranged for another relative to meet us at the docks. And they put us-- actually was a she, a woman and her mother-- put us on a train to go to Springfield where we've been ever since.

speaker: interviewer
text: Springfield, what state?

speaker: interviewee
text: Springfield, Massachusetts.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the date that you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: July 2nd, 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any recollections of your-- your first moments or day here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember coming here on a train, and getting some ice cream on the train. And we arrived here, and were met by cousins that we had in Springfield. They were first cousins of my mother, and also an aunt and uncle of my mother's that had come here in the early 1900s. And we were met at the train, and we were brought to their home in Springfield. And, of course, that was two days before 4th of July. And I remember, you know, sitting in a house where everybody, of course, was kind of becoming acquainted. And all the other cousins came to the house to meet us. And I remember being fed tuna fish for the first time, and also my being somewhat jittery about hearing all the fireworks going off. At first I would kind of jump. And my parents, too, were kind of nervous every time we would hear those noises. I guess that's the only time I remember actually being specifically nervous about something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know English at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Very little, just maybe a couple of words.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn English?

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned during the summer of 1949, from July to September. There were some cousins that were about the same age as I am. That we kind of hung out and I remember I learned English through the summer enough to be placed in sixth grade at Armor Street School in Springfield.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did other people-- say, teachers or new friends-- ask you about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Most were very curious. Of course, my English was not that good at the time. And I certainly remember, the-- in junior high school, especially, the teachers would ask me to relate my experiences, if you will. And I remember one o being written up in a newspaper at the time. We still have the articles. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember most-- in sixth grade, the teachers name was Mr. McDowell. He was-- everybody was very nice to me. I remember that. And they were very kind, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What effects would you say this experience, altogether, has had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on your parents in terms of how they raise you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think, I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] even though I was quite young, I always was part of the decision-making processes that took place within our family-- my father and my mother, myself, of course, before being married. I was-- I learned the language much quicker, so I was kind of like an interpreter for my parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was much more involved in the general household taking care of, and the decision-making process. I think my parents were also more possessive, in a sense; more protective of me. And, in a sense, that kind of made it into a very, you know, closely knit unit. Of course there were only three of us.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I think it was very beneficial to me, later in life, being involved in everything that went on within-- you know, more so than children are now involved in the decision-making processes within the household to a point, you know, where my parents couldn't write English very well, so I used to do the writing. And, of course, I would-- they would go to the store, I would be interpreting for them. And you know, certainly when my father went into business on his own, I was his-- even though I was only 13 years old, I became his bookkeeper and secretary, if you will, for as long as he was in business. Even when I was in college, I still used to do that for him. And so I think that had a great deal of my being, maybe, more mature at certain stages of my life than maybe other youngsters the same age.

speaker: interviewer
text: What effects, if any, do you think your experiences have had on the way you have raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I certainly try to give my children everything that was available to them, without spoiling them, of course. I think I feel much closer, or a very, you know, closeness to my children, as does my wife too. Of course, we were-- they were raised at different times. But I think I try to relate to them to be more appreciative of things, as certainly I have learned over the years, that, you know, when you run the gamut from having absolutely nothing to having all the luxuries, if you will, of today, I think I certainly still remember having nothing and having everything that we have now. And I think, at times, I would try to relate that to my children so that, perhaps, they would appreciate everything that they have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you been active in religious organizations in this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have. Certainly during my married life, we belonged to synagogues-- two synagogues, as a matter of fact, one that we've belonged to since we came to this country and my family belonged to. And that's an orthodox synagogue called Kesser Israel in Springfield. And after we got married, we were married in a conservative synagogue called Temple Beth El. And we've been members also all our married lives there. My children became bar mitzvah there, and we still maintain our membership there, as well. And I've been active. During the years as my children got older, I was active in the Hebrew school [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where they attended afternoon Hebrew school. And I've also been active in the-- on a committee, for a number of years, that observes Yom HaShoah in the spring. And I've been on the boards of the synagogues and-- and the various Jewish organizations.

speaker: interviewer
text: What has been your primary occupation as an adult?

speaker: interviewee
text: A pharmacist, and I owned my own pharmacies for a number of years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm married. My wife's name is Barbara-- Barbara Lee. And her maiden name was Trager-- T-R-A-G-E-R. And I have three sons-- Stephen, Michael, and David.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: One on the way. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think about the Shoah often?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. At different times, I think about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what happened. And certain-- sometimes watching television or seeing something will remind me of an incident, perhaps, mostly through what we discussed with my parents and so forth. And it would remind me about something. And-- and so I think hardly a day goes by that you don't remember some phase of your past.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you want to tell your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's important that other people, as time goes on, and perhaps all the survivors are no longer with us, what a terrible part of history that it was. And I think it's important for the world to appreciate, although, as you read the papers now and you watch the news, similar things happening in other parts of the world. And perhaps, that they said, never again, and it's still happening. So I think-- as a lesson to humanity, I think it's important that, I think, young people especially find it difficult to believe something that they didn't experience. I think it kind of goes back, you asking me earlier whether, you know, it made me a different person than I am, perhaps, and thanks to the experience. And I think to know it is to believe it. And if you don't know it, you don't believe it. So I think it's very important that these stories are-- that these are the truths of actual people that lived it. Of course, we have video now and everything, and I think this is a good way to teach future generations about history and how inhumane one person can be towards another.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any final statement you'd like to make, or is there anything you'd like to add that we did not cover?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's just to express my appreciation to the Shoah committee for doing this on a worldly basis. I know certain recordings have been done in the past, oral histories that were taken from my parents. But this project being of the magnitude that it is, it's a tremendous undertaking. And I think the world can be for the better of it. And I certainly appreciate them doing it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gerry, this concludes our interview. Thank you very much for your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape is rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't want to show this picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. Can you stop?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry. I just-- This is a picture of my father and my mother when they were married before the war. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture that was taken on the day of the dedication of the monument that my father was instrumental in putting up over the mass grave of the-- where the Jews from Mlynow and Morawica were killed. You can see the fence surrounding the general area, as well as the monument and the candelabras, which belonged to my mother, incidentally. And the person standing to our left and to the right of the monument, immediately next to the monument, is my father. And I'm also in the picture, although it's hard to see where I am. But I'm leaning over one of the posts in front.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken a short period, maybe a month or two, after we were liberated by the Russians. It, of course, was in the general area of Mlynow and Morawica. And the soldiers that you see in the picture are obviously the Russian soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture that was taken in a DP camp in Pocking, right next to the barrack that we lived in. You can see the window from the barrack, as well as the little hut that was built by my father next to the window on the outside, where we kept the chickens and other things. In that picture, in the front row going from our left to right is my cousin, Anshel. And next to him, the girl is Aviva, my adopted sister. And she's holding another cousin of mine that was born in the DP camp, Charles-- or Hanina Epstein. And the boy in the white shirt is myself. And in the rear row, going again from left to right, a lady named [? Liba ?] who also lib-- emigrated to the United States and lived in Baltimore, Maryland. Next to her, the man in the hat is my Uncle Max. Next to him in the middle is my mother. And next to my mother is my father. And the lady next to him, I don't remember her name. And then the person next to her is my uncle, [? Itzhak, ?] who was married to my Aunt Bunya. Again, the next row, from back to front, is my Aunt [? Shaindel, ?] who was married to my Uncle Max. And the lady next to her, I don't remember the name. And the bald gentlemen, I don't remember his name. However, next to him is my Aunt Bunya. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This picture shows the group called the Betar that young people belong to. And it was a young Zionist group, of course, for Israel. And it shows you the uniforms that we wore, as well. And I'm also in that picture, being in-- from-- going from right to left, I'm on the second row, the third person in with a cap.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was also-- this was taken in DP Camp Pocking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture taken in-- in the school. Apparently it was an affair that was taking place. Some of our teachers are shown there. And I'm in that picture on the left-hand side, the fifth person with a cap on. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture was taken also at the DP camp in Pocking. And it shows, from left to right, my cousin Anshel, or Albert, and my sister-- adopted sister, Aviva, and myself, again in the white shirt. And the little boy who was in front of Aviva is my cousin Charles Epstein. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a picture of myself during the winter time, again in DP Camp Pocking. And it also shows, in the background, what the barracks looked like. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was maybe seven or eight years old there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the green card, if you will, that was issued to me when we came to the United States. And it has my picture and my signature on it, signing it [INAUDIBLE] Steinberg. And I've saved it all these years. And that was given to us sometime after July 2nd, 1949, here in Springfield.

speaker: interviewer
text: Show this one here.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this time? Sure. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this picture, can you tell us about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken a short time after we came to the United States. It was taken at Forest Park in Springfield, and it's a picture of my father and my mother and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you, approximately, here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd say maybe 10 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a family picture taken in the DP camp in-- in Pocking. And it was taken a short time before we emigrated to the United States, possibly late 1948 or early 1949. It shows my mother and my father and myself and my adopted sister, Aviva, in the front. And this was taken as a family portrait before she left for Israel and we left for the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape's rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Here we have Gerry Steinberg and his wife, Barbara Steinberg. And Gerry and Barbara, do you have anything that you'd like to conclude with? Is there anything that you'd like to say at this moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, unfortunately, we cannot have my three sons here today. But they're here in spirit. And I'm certainly very happy to have my wife of 32 years, almost 33, here with me, who's been a great supporter of me over the years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And again, we both appreciate this opportunity to record a piece of history.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, both of you.
